sections.title: What comparatives & superlatives do (core logic)
sections.content:
**Comparatives** compare **two** things (*taller than her brother*); **superlatives** pick the extreme in a group of 3+ (*the tallest in the class*).
- Comparison is how English **grades adjectives** — part of the Noun Phrase root (#3 in the Tree Model), because the adjective is modifying a noun.
- Every opinion, description, recommendation and exam writing task leans on it: *better*, *worse*, *cheaper*, *the most useful*. Without comparatives you can describe one thing; with them you can rank, choose, and argue.

items.label: Long adjectives
items.formula: more / most → more useful, the most useful

sections.title: The form rules — short vs long adjectives
sections.content:
The big split — the single most important rule in this topic.
- **Short** = 1 syllable, or 2 ending in *-y* → add **-er / -est**. **Long** = 2+ syllables, usually → **more / most**.
Get the split right and the rest follows.

diagrams.title: Comparative & superlative forms
rows: expensive → more expensive → the most expensive

items.why: mixing the two camps is the #1 error

sections.title: Irregular forms — the must-know list
sections.content:
A small group of high-frequency adjectives ignore the rules — learn them as a set.
These are some of the most-used words in English: *good*, *bad* and *much/many* alone account for a huge slice of everyday comparison.

sections.title: than after comparatives
sections.content:
The structure: **X + be + comparative + than + Y** — and *than* is obligatory.
Pronoun choice: informal English uses *than me / than him* (*She's taller than me*); formal English prefers *than I / than he* (*She's taller than I am*). Both are accepted in B1–B2 writing.

items.label: Pattern
items.formula: X + be + comparative + than + Y → She's taller than her brother.

items.label: than + clause
items.formula: The meeting took longer than we expected.

items.good: This film is **more interesting than** the book.

items.why: one of the most common errors — never drop *than*

diagrams.text: *než* maps cleanly onto *than*, but don't drop it — *than* is obligatory.

sections.title: (not) as … as — equality and softer comparison
sections.content:
A second comparison pattern, alongside *-er / more*.
Native speakers reach for *not as … as* constantly because it sounds less blunt than the opposite comparative. Also a classic exam trap.

left.title: Equal: as … as
points: She's **as tall as** her sister. (same height)
*just as / nearly as / almost as* — degrees of equality

right.title: Unequal: not as … as
points: She's **not as tall as** her brother. = he is taller (softer than *shorter than*)
This exam **wasn't as hard as** the last one.

diagrams.text: Common Czech L1 error: *not so tall like her brother* — the pattern is **not as … as**, never *like*.

sections.title: less / least — the opposite direction
sections.content:
**less / least** = the reverse of *more / most* — mainly used with **long adjectives**.
For long adjectives, *less* is the only way to say "in the opposite direction" — there's no *-er* alternative.

items.good: This phone is **less expensive** than that one.
It's **the least difficult** question in the test.
He's **less interested** in football than his brother.

items.why: *less tall*, *less big* sound odd with short adjectives — save *less* for long ones

sections.title: Advanced patterns (B2)
sections.content:
Higher-level moves that turn basic comparison into natural, exam-grade English — these structures separate B1 from B2 in speaking and writing; examiners listen for them.
Degree modifiers go **before** the comparative, never with the bare adjective.

diagrams.title: Degree modifiers — how much of a difference

items.label: Degree modifier
items.formula: modifier + comparative → much taller, far more expensive, a bit older, slightly cheaper

items.label: The … the … (parallel change)
items.formula: The older he gets, the wiser he becomes. / The more you practise, the better you get.

items.label: Repeated comparative (increasing change)
items.formula: It's getting bigger and bigger. / Prices are going up and up.

diagrams.text: *The more you practise, the better you get* — Czech *čím víc … tím víc …*.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
High-frequency mistakes to drill out — they appear in every gap-fill, error-correction, and writing task.
Fixing them gives immediate visible progress.

items.bad: not so tall like her brother

items.why: when comparing English with English — compare like with like

sections.title: Practice roadmap & next steps
sections.content:
Direct practice in this curriculum — start here after the cards:
- Gap fill – basic (form the comparative)
- Advanced comparatives (modifiers, *the … the …*, *not as … as*)
- Superlatives (the + -est / the most)
- Error correction (the doubles and the *than* drops)

Full irregular list at **Reference → Comparatives and Superlatives**.

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?

options.a: This exercise is easier than the last one.
options.b: This exercise is more easier than the last one.
options.c: This exercise is more easy than the last one.
options.d: This exercise is easyer than the last one.

questions.explanation: Easy → easier. We cannot use both more + adjective and -er together.

questions.question: Choose the correct comparative.
questions.explanation: Tall is a short adjective → taller than.

questions.question: Which is the correct form?
questions.explanation: Interesting is a long adjective → more interesting than.

questions.question: Complete the sentence: 'His English is ____ mine.'
questions.explanation: Good → better (irregular comparative).

questions.question: Which negative comparison is correct?

options.a: She is not so tall like her brother.
options.b: She is not so taller as her brother.
options.c: She is not as tall like her brother.
options.d: She is not as tall as her brother.

questions.explanation: The pattern is not as + adjective + as (not so ... like is a common mistake).

gapfill.title: Practice: Gap fill – Form the comparative (basic)

questions.question: 1. My sister is ____ (tall) me.
questions.explanation: Tall → taller (short adjective + -er) + than.

questions.question: 2. This exercise is ____ (easy) the last one.
questions.explanation: Easy → easier (y → ier).

questions.question: 3. London is ____ (big) Prague.
questions.explanation: Big → bigger (double the final consonant).

questions.question: 4. Today is ____ (hot) yesterday.
questions.explanation: Hot → hotter (double the final consonant).

questions.question: 5. This task is ____ (interesting) the others.
questions.explanation: Interesting is long → more interesting than.

questions.question: 6. His English is ____ (good) mine.
questions.explanation: Good → better.

questions.question: 7. The situation is ____ (bad) before.
questions.explanation: Bad → worse.

questions.question: 8. This street is ____ (narrow) the main road.
questions.explanation: Narrow → narrower (add -er).

questions.question: 9. The film was ____ (boring) I expected.
questions.explanation: Boring is long → more boring than.

questions.question: 10. My flat is ____ (far) from the centre than yours.
questions.explanation: Far → farther/further.

questions.question: 11. This exam is ____ (difficult) the last one.
questions.explanation: Difficult is long → more difficult than.

questions.question: 12. She is ____ (old) she looks. (She looks younger than she is.)
questions.explanation: Old → older + than. Context: she looks younger, so she is older than she looks.

questions.question: 13. This book is ____ (far / interesting) the film.
questions.explanation: Use a modifier (far/much) + more interesting than.

questions.question: 14. The new system is ____ (a bit / efficient) the old one.
questions.explanation: Use a bit/slightly + more efficient than.

questions.question: 15. Rewrite using 'not as ... as': 'Her car is cheaper than mine.'
answers: My car is not as cheap as hers.
My car isn't as cheap as hers.
questions.explanation: Cheaper than → my car is not as cheap as hers.

questions.question: 16. Rewrite using 'the more ... the more ...': 'If you practise more, your English will get better.'
answers: The more you practise, the better your English gets.
The more you practise, the better your English will get.
questions.explanation: If you practise more, your English will get better → The more you practise, the better your English (will) get(s).

questions.question: 17. Complete: 'The ____ (soon) we start, the ____ (soon) we can finish.'
answers: The sooner we start, the sooner we can finish.
the sooner we start, the sooner we can finish
questions.explanation: The sooner ..., the sooner ...

questions.question: 18. Complete: 'He drives ____ (carefully) he used to.'
questions.explanation: Carefully is an adverb → more carefully than ...

questions.question: 19. The meeting took ____ (long) we expected.
questions.explanation: Comparative with than + clause: longer than we expected.

questions.question: 20. This year's results are ____ (slightly / good) last year's.
questions.explanation: Use a small degree adverb + better (slightly/a bit better than).

questions.question: 21. She is ____ (tall) girl in the class.
questions.explanation: Tall → the tallest (superlative with the).

questions.question: 22. This is ____ (good) restaurant in town.
questions.explanation: Good → the best (irregular superlative).

questions.question: 23. That was the ____ (bad) exam I've ever done.
questions.explanation: Superlative of bad → worst (the worst exam).

questions.question: 24. It’s ____ (interesting) book I've ever read.
answers: the most interesting book I've ever read.
the most interesting book I have ever read.
the most interesting book I’ve ever read.
questions.explanation: Long adjective → the most interesting (superlative) + I've ever read.

questions.question: 25. January is usually ____ (cold) month of the year here.
questions.explanation: Cold → the coldest.

questions.question: 26. This question is ____ (difficult) one in the test.
questions.explanation: Difficult → the most difficult (long adjective).

questions.question: 27. Of all the villages around here, he lives in ____ (far) from the city.
questions.explanation: Superlative (comparing all villages): far → the farthest / the furthest.

questions.question: 28. This is one of ____ (expensive) hotels in the city.
questions.explanation: Pattern: one of the + superlative plural → one of the most expensive hotels.

questions.question: 1. Wrong: 'This exercise is more easier than the last one.' Correct the sentence.
answers: This exercise is easier than the last one.
This exercise is easier than the last one
questions.explanation: Don't use more + -er together. Just easier.

questions.question: 2. Wrong: 'He is more tall than his brother.' Correct the sentence.
answers: He is taller than his brother.
He is taller than his brother
questions.explanation: Tall is short → taller than, not more tall than.

questions.question: 3. Wrong: 'Today is more cold than yesterday.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Cold is short → colder than, not more cold than.

questions.question: 4. Wrong: 'Prague is more small than London.' Correct the sentence.
questions.explanation: Small is short → smaller than, not more small than.

questions.question: 5. Wrong: 'This problem is worser than the last one.' Correct the sentence.
answers: This problem is worse than the last one.
This problem is worse than the last one
questions.explanation: Bad → worse, not worser.

questions.question: 6. Wrong: 'She is not so tall like her brother.' Correct the sentence.
answers: She is not as tall as her brother.
She is not as tall as her brother
questions.explanation: Pattern: not as + adjective + as (not so ... like).

questions.question: 7. Wrong: 'This road is the more dangerous than that one.' Correct the sentence.
answers: This road is more dangerous than that one.
This road is more dangerous than that one
questions.explanation: Comparative: more dangerous than (no the before a simple comparative).

questions.question: 8. Wrong: 'Her English is better than my sister.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Her English is better than my sister's.
Her English is better than my sister's English.
questions.explanation: We compare English with English: better than my sister's (English).
